topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure for the new city lay crumpled on the coffee table, the vibrant cityscape mocking the quiet ache in my chest. I traced the outline of a distant skyline with a fingertip, the paper feeling flimsy beneath my touch. Liam had casually mentioned it over lukewarm pizza last week, a breezy announcement about a new job opportunity. Now, the casualness of it felt like a physical blow, a sudden drop in temperature. I stared at the framed photo of us laughing on the beach, the memory suddenly sharp, painfully real. Every shared inside joke, every late-night confession, suddenly seemed weighted with an impending distance. I picked up my phone, the screen illuminating the raw knot tightening in my stomach, and hovered over his contact, my thumb trembling above the call button, unsure if I had the strength to bridge the miles they were about to create.

[story 2]

It was the silence in my apartment that screamed the news. The usual comforting rhythm of our shared space—the faint hum of the refrigerator, the way his worn armchair always sat by the window—was suddenly hollow. I kept replaying the conversation in my head, the way his smile had faltered just a fraction when he dropped the bombshell. My hands started to feel clammy, slick with a sudden, unwelcome sweat. I paced the length of the living room three times, the worn rug feeling strangely alien under my bare feet. Each step was heavy, weighted down by the realization that the familiar landscape of my life was about to shift irrevocably. I sank onto the sofa, pulling my knees to my chest, wishing the floor would just swallow me whole, needing a moment where I didn't have to hold anything together.

[story 3]

"So, you're actually leaving?" The words tasted like dust in my mouth, dry and sharp. I watched his eyes, searching for any flicker of deceit, but found only a deep, resigned sadness that mirrored the tightening in my own throat. I managed a small, brittle laugh, a sound that didn't reach my eyes. The sheer logistics of it—the packing, the new lease, the sheer geographical separation—felt overwhelming, a tidal wave rising without warning. I started fiddling obsessively with the loose thread on my sweater, my gaze fixed on the pattern, anything to avoid looking at the reality of the empty space that was rapidly expanding between us. I felt a strange lightness, as if my own foundation had just been subtly undermined, leaving me unsteady on my feet.
